speaker: Rob Moore
words: We can just start wherever you want to start.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Okay, is it going? Let's go. My name is Lela Grace Jain Wicks. I am the daughter of Walter Farwell Jain and Mary Lela Lanphear Jain, pioneers of Nez Perce County and Latah County. My father came as a little boy to the Palouse with his father who was born in Switzerland in a very beautiful manner on the edge of the city of Morges, M-O-R-G-E-S, Switzerland. At the age of four in 1847, he and his three brothers, his father and mother, arrived in New York via sailing vessel. His father, William Benjamin Jain, was financed and repeatedly helped to establish a home in the New World because Jains, like other families of some means, always sent their sons to other countries than the small nation of Switzerland for the greater opportunities to make their futures. This family of Jains were greatly aware of the freedom offered by the new nation and were impatient under the restrictions of their native country. They did not have large means because my great-grandfather had married for love and a minister's daughter, Rose Mennet, M-E-double-N-E-T. His name, by the way, was J-A-I-N with an umlaut or two dots over the I. It was pronounced almost as though there was a Y in the middle of the name. At home, that is, till it sounded like J-O-Y-E-N. In the ankle-sized form, however, it was the short Jain. The dowry that usually went in marriages of the family was absent because the minister father of Rose Mennet was not a moneyed man. The family stayed in New York rather briefly and then went on to Wisconsin where they settled near Vernon in Dane County. The Genesee News of Friday, May 6, 1938 carries an interview of my father which reads as follows. Sure. Walter F. Jain, a resident of Genesee for 60 years, recalls early days in the blues country and in reminiscence paused to tell of the trip overland with his parents, Mr. and Mrs. Louis Jain. May I parenthesize here to say that his name is spelled L-E-W-I-S, but of course his name was French and was spelled L-O-U-I-S, which apparently was an embarrassment to him because legal documents always carry his signature as L period Jain. However, on his tombstone he is written as L-E-W-I-S. Close parenthesis. He came with his parents and his sister Grace from the family home of Vernon, Wisconsin to Boulder, Colorado, which they pronounce Colorado.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Immigrants to the west were not given to a consideration of time when traveling. It was their custom to start out in the spring expecting to reach their destination in the fall. Such was the case with the Jain family. They left Vernon in May 1871 with two teams, one of horses and a span of mules, one of which was driven by Charles Farwell, a brother of Mrs. Louis Jain. Her name was Adelia. On the long trek across the prairies, the Platte River stretched out for miles across Nebraska and into Colorado, paralleling the route taken by many westbound families. They did not travel when the organized train of new settlers and their purpose in making the trip was not to tour the country, but to seek new lands and there establish a home. Walter Jain recalls that he was a boy of four years, but he vividly remembers the trip. Living six years near Boulder, Colorado, two other children were born, Charles and Carrie. Some of the land on which the elder Mr. Jain settled for a time is now a part of the property of the Colorado Teachers College at Boulder. Grasshoppers that swarmed over the Midwest states and into Colorado between 1871 and 1876 were most depressing and caused a number of the early settlers to move farther west. There are two sweet little stories that father told us from this time, both of which center around the scarcity of fresh fruit for which the family had a keen craving. One of the stories is that grandmother gave him a little bucket and even at the age of six he was allowed to go alone up the mountain to pick wild berries. By the time he had eaten all that the little boy would and by the time he had bounced around all that the little boy would, he came home with about two tablespoons of juice in the bottom of that bucket, but it was a source of great pride to him and his mother bragged about him greatly as to his being a gleaner of food. The other story was of their going to town for a special day and of his father giving him a nickel with which he bought one peach. It was just a wrinkled green skin over a hard old pit, but he ate it with relish and sucked the pit the rest of the day. The Jain family left Colorado in 1877 for stopping in the Grand Ronde Valley where they were enticed into adjoining Indian Valley where some land was taken up. The father, like all pioneers, sought places where they might earn a few dollars and this urge took him into the Walla Walla country for the harvest season. I wish to interpolate here that a cute little story that comes from his school days in the Grand Ronde. There was a shallow stream near the school house and the salmon came up to spawn and would do their best to get across the ford which was very shallow indeed. One of these huge creatures came along and looked like a real prize to the children and Dad, with his own kind of enterprise, frightened that fish to turn and swim itself right on to the dry road bed. He fell upon it belly down and kept it there until it died and he could take it home to his mother where again he was bragged about.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Another story from this interlude was of a pack rat that annoyed grandmother, no end, and it was up in the rafters. Well one day Dad got a good look at it, grabbed his father's gun and shot it. Of course the remains of the poor beast settled down over everything and father never learned to shoot anything that was right above you. To get back to the walla walla portion of the tale, other harvest hands who had traveled more or less spoke highly of the new Palouse country and so a year later in 1878 the Jain family was on its way to Genesee. The teams which brought them across the Great Plains were replaced by four broncos, one that served as a saddle mount while the other three were practically unbroken when they left Colorado. In Oregon many of the early settlers found oxen better suited for general beasts of burden with plowing to do. With the knowledge gained the Jain family headed, the Jain family headed around giving two horses for four oxen and by the time they were ready to head for Idaho the livestock inventory was four oxen, two milk cows, two calves and a saddle horse. The latter soon after arriving in Idaho reverted to his native habits and became a member of a herd of wild horses that was often seen roaming the country. The first camp made by the Jain family in Idaho was on the Thompson place south of Genesee. A granddaughter of that family and I are dear friends. Her name is Emma Sherrod, Mrs. Fred Sherrod. Oh yes, a member of the Thompson family was a daughter, Miss Johanna Thompson who later became Mrs. Christian and is the mother of Mrs. Fred Sherrod with whom she makes her home. This is 1938. Continuing toward what is now Genesee the family saw that a number of settlers were already in the valley and immediately Mr. Jain preempted the place now owned by Mike Reisenauer, R-E-I-S-E-N-A-U-E-R east of Genesee and a few years later homesteaded the place now farmed by Don Jain, a son of Walter Jain. After the arrival of the Jain family at Genesee a third son, Clarence, was born. The house that they lived in still stands in very bad condition. Clarence was born in one corner of the front room and little Carrie, aged 15 in 1891, died in yet another corner of that same room. To continue with the write up. Having lost the saddle bronc or cow use, Mr. Jain had no further use for the saddle which he brought along so he drove to Lewiston and there gave up a prized possession as part payment on a $40 breaking pile. Settlers were beginning to break up sod but in some instances homesteaders were without horses or oxen and their shortage afforded a source of income for the elder Mr. Jain and his son Walter. The going price for breaking sod was $2.50 per acre and with oxen to pull the breaking plow the net income was gratifying. Oxen were able to rustle their feed from the abundant growth of bunch grass while horses to stand the heavy work required some grain. I might interpolate here that father said his 11 year and 12 year old heart nearly burst with pity because the little prairie chickens hatched in the bunch grass were terribly frightened and dismayed by the inroads of the breaking plow and they would run over the furrows to the unbroken portions inside the area to be plowed and then would be plowed under.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: And he would run and gather them up whenever he could catch them and take them back to safety on the other side of the furrows but they would almost beat him back to their original place and would get killed. He said the prairie chickens were so numerous that on a haystack in the fall they would frequently land there and roost or rest and the hay would be covered with their gray bodies. A home was built on what is now the Reisenauer place. More acres were broken out and the more enterprising settlers began advancing upward on the hills with their farming operations. Threshing outfits became common and a market was found for small grains at Lewiston. The principal crop was wheat that sold for four bits a bushel and was used entirely for bread and feed locally, there being no explorable surplus at the time. I wish to interpolate here before we get too far from that first entrance into the country to tell two things that dad spoke of most feelingly. One was of his extreme fatigue when as a little boy he would be given the full load of a working man as partner to his father in driving a span of oxygen. He would hook his arm over the horn of the nigh oxen and walk along by him, his bare feet in the dust, and sleep. He swore he could rest greatly in this way because the oxen would just, all he had to do was just barely move his feet because the oxen would take him. And he was a friendly old beast that helped the little boy know it. The other story which I find very touching is of grandfather, a sturdy little square, French-Swiss that he was, taking a bait, B-A-I-T, of grain down to his stock which he wintered on the hat way. But he was afraid they might get too thin so he would carry once or twice a week some grain on his own sturdy back down to feed his animals. And that way got them through the winter in good shape so they'd be ready for work in the spring. This meant a walk of better than ten miles through the deep snow in the dead of winter in North Idaho. Quite a feat. For sure. To continue, with the mining industry opening up more each day, the miners were heavy buyers. Wheat was transported by wagon down the Lewiston Hill, some taking a grave that led to a ferry a short distance below the power dam on the Clearwater.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: While others, this is the old W-W-P dam he is speaking of which was demolished in 1973. While others took a route that brought them to the foot of the hill to another ferry at a point where the railroad bridge now crosses the Clearwater River. This railroad bridge has also been relocated. I might say at this point that when the family got to the Clearwater River to ford across to come up to Genesee, Granddad didn't have the twenty-five cents in cash for the ferryman's fee. And the man stood him off and in all good faith he returned later to pay the money. Ferrying teams and loads across the river required a cash outlay. And where four or more animals were used in the hauling, two animals, as a rule, took the load from the river onto the mill on the Lewiston side, thereby saving cost of ferrying the full number of animals. Yesterday I drove to Lewiston and as I went over the high ridge that starts the slope of the canyon down to the Clearwater from the north side, we crossed the tiny loop that goes into the state of Washington at the top of the Hathaway Canyon. There is located the Collins place and the story goes of Mr. Collins, who was an exceedingly strong man, having to lessen his load for his team of sacks of wheat carried to the top of the ridge. And so he took the sacks, one sixty pound sack in each hand, and carried them to the top of the ridge, one on each side. They were of such length that the arm had to be brought up to an unnatural pitch. It wasn't as though they were carried at full length but were brought up to an unnatural place. And he carried one in each hand to the top of that ridge until he lessened his load time after time after time to get that wheat to the top of the hill.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why would he be hauling wheat to the top of the hill in the first place?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: To ferry it away from Lewiston, to put it on the boat to go down the river to the portland and the shipping point.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Where was his place?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Right at the top of Hathaway. You can see he had to go south up to the top of the ridge, you see, and then go down the little old road which you can still see there. And it was a terrible task to get that heavy load held back enough to get it down that extremely steep pitch. They said the brakes would smoke and burn and have to have water poured on them. They just worked every scheme they could to hold the weight of that load from running away and destroying itself. Getting down the steep old pitch. The problems of transportation were almost as insupportable as the problems of growth in those early days, you know. To continue, in the early days settlers were somewhat reluctant to try farming all their lands. The hills seemed rather steep and as a result crops were grown on the flatter valley lands mostly. Frost took a heavy toll of ripening crops in the lower lands and it has always only been in more recent years that frosts have been unknown. As the years rolled by and markets developed and some of the homesteaders sold out, some relinquished their claims but those who remained or who came in later years witnessed the cultivation of practically every foot of their lands, which for over 60 years have produced crops that justified the toil and hardships of the pioneers. Those who remained with the country have never witnessed a crop failure. There was one year that grains could not be harvested because of too much moisture, 1893. That tried the patience of the settlers to the utmost. Some were unable to finance themselves but for the most part the early settlers stuck it out with the perseverance typical of all pioneering families. Some years after the Jain family came into the Palouse country, Walter Jain bought a relinquishment of 80 acres, which is now part of the Ed Peterson ranch farmed by Adrian Nelson. Miss Mary Lila Lanphear and her mother of Salt River, Michigan came to Genesee in 1892 to join the father and husband who came out a year earlier. His name was Joshua Giles Lanphear. They did not come from Salt River, Michigan. They came from Gladwin, Michigan. Salt River, however, was the place where my mother, Mary Lila Lanphear, was born. They lived in Gladwin where her grandfather had a meat market and grocery business. He also owned a hook factory there. But I think his fortunes fell on somewhat dramatic, ill times, over a pacer. This was a horse of great renown, but alas, the return monetarily was not equal to the occasion. And he struck out to start over again in the west when mother was just about 16. They brought many household things and lived from the packed food which they carried with them, mother and grandmother, whose name was Barbara Ann Walters, the daughter of...

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Stop it.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: To continue, Barbara Ann Walters was the daughter of Mary Ann Cable Walters, a woman who was married to Jacob Walters, a German Swiss. They lived in Pennsylvania. And Barbara Ann, at an early age, since she is a pioneer, I'll take a minute to talk about her. At age 13, she was a talented seamstress already. And at 14, she had made enough fancy white shirts for young men to have earned herself the first sewing machine to go into her area. It was then possible for her to make the elaborate shirts which had the tucked bosoms and the ruffled cuffs of the period. She was an exceedingly capable and supportive wife. While in the grocery store and meat market, she not only sold groceries, she cut meat. And I have the long, white linen aprons which she wore, bordered with handmade lace, to show that she not only was a capable meat cutter, but also a feminine housewife. When she and mother came to the country...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did they come, first of all?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: They came by train. They came by train carrying everything they were going to have with them. And as I have said, including their food. Mother was dressed in an exquisite outfit, fashioned by her mother's expert needle. It went from deep red kidge slippers, which I have, to a darling little red velvet and flower hat. And the costume ranged in colors from rose to deep wine red. It was mother's color. She was black eyed and very creamy, lovely skin. And I guess she was quite a breathtaking picture. First they came and boarded in a house which is still standing in Genesee. Just across the street west from the Emmett house, Robert and Mabel Emmett, which is still owned by Mabel Emmett and lived in by Leland Emmett in this year of 1974. That house which is directly west across the street was where grandmother and grandfather and mother boarded it first. Then they went to old town Genesee and started another grocery store. But this was short lived. Apparently their little grub steak was gone because both Joshua and Barbara went to work as a couple for William Nixon, who lived south of town on land which is still owned by his daughter, Grace Nixon. The following year, mother was much pursued by local young men and had a fabulous time. She went to high school and one of the people whom she knew and highly prized as a friend was John Platt, father of Kenneth, who has written Whispers of Old Genesee. His wife, the Batdorf girl, was also in evidence and then of course they were all just young people together. In due time, Walter Jain succeeded in winning her heart, but not before she had taken a teacher's examination in Moscow and lived in the Del Norte hotel which is on the corner of 1st and Main and in the same building in which I'm making this recording. I am in an apartment in the West End, a new section, but the old Hotel Del Norte was on the corner here and the structure is still embedded in this apartment building. This was in 1892.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: She was teaching then?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: She passed her teacher's exam and taught one term of school, but that fall, November 8th, she was married to my father in the Nixon front room. She had again an outstanding wedding outfit made of green wool with matching velvet yoke. I have seen this material and it was very handsome. The only thing that hurt her pride was when she went to town for new wedding shoes, she arrived home with one shoe from one size and one shoe from another. They were of the same make, but they were of different sizes and as long as she lived, this seemed to bother her.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What was their wedding journey?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Their wedding journey was across the about 4 miles to Dad's 12x14 cabin. This was done in his hack and since this was the fall, the wet fall of 1893, that wagon box at times scraped the mud. When they got to the home, Dad had a typical bachelor's cabin.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Where was his cabin?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: It was on the 80th, which he just mentioned preempting here on the Ed Peterson place and I can show you the location. The road to it was very circuitous because the access right was not too clear, so they had to go the long way around to get to the 80th. The furniture consisted of a bed in one corner, a table in another, a stove in a third, and some hooks on the wall. A couple of straight chairs and this was it. Well, Mother, who had been very gently brought up and had enjoyed some social standing in Michigan and certainly had been rushed beautifully in jealousy, must have found this a little different.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Hasn't she ever seen it before?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Well, I'm sure she's probably seen it. I don't know though. The proprieties in those days were a little bit more delicate than observed. Now, I don't know whether she'd ever been alone with her prospective husband in his dwelling or not, but anyway, it remained in this shape for some time until Grandmother came over one very hot afternoon in summer and determined that her beloved daughter wasn't going to stay in the condition like this, so she gathered up what materials were at hand and built a summer kitchen. This greatly enhanced the worth of the place and led to the comfort of all. And Barbara Ann did the building herself. Three weeks after Mother and Dad were married, she became pregnant. This was a joke in the family because at the time of their marriage, the banker, Fred Bresler, was also married to Bertha Hollister, daughter of the local hotel man. Fred Bresler was the banker. And they were married just three days before Mrs. Bresler became pregnant. And Fred used to tease my father because he was such a...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Can you tell us that last story?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Yeah, I'll just start again because you stop there with the children and I'll go on with the classics part now. Okay, sure. It was a very happy interlude for the family because, for Walter and Leila, because they read the classics. And they had this very nice hired man named Will Gorman, who had a problem with alcohol, but who was a man of some education. And really, the three of them dipping into the classics had a warm and delightful interlude. The fresh oysters shipped in from the coast were the greatest treat known at that time. And two friends, Bertha Hollister Bresler's sister, Edith Hollister, and her fiancée, Ed Jacobs, came to the Jain home one day and were married there. These also were lifelong dear friends and the generations go forward with still the closest of relationships. And now it's gotten to be to the sixth generation. For the wedding dinner of the Jacobs, fresh oyster stew was made with the imported oysters, which was really something. And I imagine it was extra good because, after all, they'd have all of the good country cream in the world and make a choice with it. Well, anyway, the one thing that was troublesome during that interlude was that Mother loved to write to her friends in Michigan. This was just almost necessary to her well-being. But for Dad to scramble around and find the cash for that numerous amount of postage was really something. They had lots of food and all of the necessities. But to find the cash for things was just quite a problem.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How old were they each?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Father was 26 and Mother was 17, but she was 18 in just 33 days after she was married. So her first son was born August 30th of 1894. And his name was Lewis, L-E-W-I-S, Giles Jain. Her second child was born also while they lived on this land. He came in May of 1896, May 30th. And his name was Benjamin Willis Jain. The third child, born also in this place, was Gladys Leah Jain, who later married James McGee. She was born February 2nd, 1896. The family then sold this land to Mr. Peterson and moved to town until Dad could relocate. They lived in the Bundy House, which is presently across the street from the property of Jack McGee, oldest son of Gladys and James. It is just slightly south and across the street from where he presently owns his home. In the Bundy House, two events occurred of significance in family history. One was a severe illness of Walter, from which he never fully recovered his youthful vigor. The other was the birth of Don, Donald Richason, R-I-C-H-A-S-O-N, Jain. He was named for Mother's grandfather, Richason Lanphear, a man who lived in Verona, New York, a farmer and cheesemaker. Donald was just a little fellow when Dad relocated the family on land that he bought the preemption and also bought some land outright at the top of Catholic Gulch. This land is still in the family and is presently in the name of Lewis Jain. There, in 1906, I was born. My name was Lela for my mother, Grace for Dad's beloved sister, Jain. Later, I married Guy Wicks, so my name is Lela Grace Jain Wicks, all one syllable. Now, dare I return to this write-up my father gave the tendency to? The parents of Mrs. Jain, after a few years at Genesee, located in the Deary country, then moved to near what was known as Hog Meadow near Helmer, and when they retired from ranching moved to Troy, where they resided for a number of years. When buried, they were laid to rest in Burnt Ridge Cemetery.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: This is your grandparents?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: My mother's people.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why did they end up in Burnt Ridge Cemetery?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Because it's the closest one to the town of Troy, a very lovely one. I go there every year to decorate their graves and I bless the pioneers for their foresight in choosing such beautiful, beautiful spots in which to lay their loved ones. I have yet to find a cemetery that was not chosen with some care, with an idea to beauty and tranquility. To go back to Dad's write-up here, he says, on November 8, 1893, Walter Jain and Miss Lanphear were married, built a home on the 80 acres and made it their home for nine years. Later they sold the 80 and with a decided influx of settlers, land was becoming scarce, so they purchased a larger place six miles southeast of Genesee, where Mr. and Mrs. Lou Jain now live and ranch. In the year of 1910, the family decided to move to Genesee and for 28 years, Mr. and Mrs. Walter Jain have lived in their present home. For a few winters while the parents of Mr. Jain were in California, Mr. and Mrs. Walter Jain occupied the residence of the Elder Jains, the property now owned by Robert Amott. This place was built by Lewis and Nadilia Jain early in the century and they said it was made strong enough to be a greenery because Grandfather insisted on all of the boards being diagonal and double-faced so that everything was really going to last. And I guess there hasn't been a sag in the old dwelling yet.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: There still are people living in it.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Oh yes, Leland Evans lives in it right now. This is on a hill and later on they moved where they wouldn't have to climb the hill and the location of that house is where the Robinsons live presently and it's on the just exactly east of the park next to the alley and facing Main Street. The next door to them and what was the Glen Sapsen property for many years was where their son Clarence and his wife Lula Jain lived for many years. The first thing that I remember about the wedding cake and events of great importance to the family was the celebration in that house of the 50th wedding anniversary of Lewis and Nadilia. I remember it so well because the wedding cake was made by the four granddaughters. My big sister Gladys made the first layer which was white. My cousin Amsel Jain, daughter of Charles and his wife Jessie Miller Jain, made the next layer which was golden. My cousin Olive Elizabeth Jain made the next layer which was white and I made the tiny top layer which was gold. Then when the time came for us to eat our dinner, I and a little adopted cousin who didn't remain with the family, Billy, who was adopted by Charles and Jessie, were sent next door to eat because children were seen and not heard and children were fed after the adults and I never saw the cake cut. I don't remember eating a bite of it and I was so full of tears being banished that to this day I can remember it with nothing but dismay. And it's been all these getting towards 60 years. But it was an event of great importance and grandmother had a white wedding gown and orange blossoms sent from California. This was on Valentine's Day of 1915 or 1916. I guess it was 1915. Invitations were sent in the form of Cupids to the whole country and people came all day for the reception in the afternoon. Grandmother wore a beautiful black satin gown and everyone came, brought beautiful gifts and it was a day of great pleasure. Grandmother had a cute little brown bun on the back of her head formed by her own hair. She loved to have it combed and to her delight she would take off her apron and put it over her shoulders and tie the strings under her chin and allow whatever willing grandchild was at hand to comb her hair.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Of course the girls would get fancy and would use water to make it stay in place and would use the curling iron to put a crimp in it. I don't know how many times her shoulders were soaked or how many times her scalp was burned but she never complained and the children took enormous delight in this past time. She was a darling with children. She was a tiny little woman with black eyes who couldn't hold us on her lap because she was never well. I remember sitting on my grandfather's lap however and putting my legs on either side so that I faced him and burying my nose in his neck because he had a long beard and I remember the scratch of the beard and the smell of him which I thought just delicious. I loved him very deeply. He was very positive but once in the blue moon his lips would come out of those grey grizzled bushes and give me a nice kiss. Their house was located close to the school of course because I went to school in the same structure which still stands and played over every inch of that east set of steps and entrance. I'd frequently come down and drop in at grandmothers on my way home. It was usually good for a cookie from a cookie jar of endless supply and once in a while when it seemed propitious I'd be invited for dinner. To the Pioneer Jains a very good meal was boiled potatoes, fried bacon, the juice of the bacon drizzled generously over those potatoes with plenty of salt and pepper, some of grandmothers good homemade bread or maybe hot biscuits with butter homemade and honey or maybe sorghum and a cup of tea. To the child of course there was always a cup of water. I never remember drinking a glass of milk. That was a delicious meal and we usually wound up with maybe one of the cookies or some cake. To us it stung like a starchy diet but to them it was par. And I thought I was greatly privileged to get to share. I also stopped in next door where from time to time to send the children to school the Charles Jains lived. Aunt Jess was a famous cook and I got gourmet food when I was lucky enough to catch a meal there. I did this quite often because I used their piano to practice on. Our folks still had mother's organ which had been imported from Michigan being one of the very first in the state and I still presently have the organ still to that instrument. The organ was sold along with mother's driving horse in order to buy me a piano. It was a cable Nelson and still is in existence in the Lloyd Wilson home. The Maytag washer was bought about the same time as the piano and it was purchased with the sale of Gladys riding horse which had some so called hot blood and was quite an invaluable animal. So the two women felt that they traded their farm animals to very good advantage in their new home in town. Now I will go back to the... Children born to Mr. and Mrs. Walter Jain were and I have listed those.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You're in life's trade.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Yes. Those who will recall the early days in this part of Idaho when Walter Jain came here as a boy and who Mr. Jain remembers as young people were members of the Sharon Horse family.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was that your grandfather who left Switzerland with his parents?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Yes, in 1847 he was aged four.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Now his father was William Benjamin Jain.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: No, his father was.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: His father was William.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: His name was Lewis.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I got that down here. He said he was impatient under the restrictions of the time.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Yes, William Benjamin was.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What were those restrictions?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Switzerland had had a very bad time and I don't know just the chronology here. By the domination of the people of Bern called the Bernese and just before that the Bernese had thrown off the yoke of the Duke of Savoy who was a tyrant in their eyes. The Bernese then dominated and Morges, the family seat at this time, was and still so, was in the canton of Vaud, V-A-U-D. The Vaudois wished to have freedom and be on their own as it were. This was brought about and some of the Jains were involved in financing and in actual carrying of arms to bring about this change. So that the Vaudois people became one of a number of cantons all on an equal basis in the nation of Switzerland. In 1972 I went back to get the family narrative and was told that I had a Vaudois face. V-A-U-D-O-I-S-E. Vaudois face.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: In particular did they, did you ever hear stories about did they restrict his movements or did they...

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Oh yes, we had the whole family narrative. At times this became a little hairy. One letter from one ancestor to his nephew says, change your name and move. Aristocrats were not in too great safety you see. Besides when they got busy and gathered up money and actually promoted arms and naturally the status quo would be a little... Antagonistic. Of course this was during the French Revolution, the American Revolution. Our history in Switzerland dates from August 24th 1572 when the family legend goes that a boy age 17 escaped from the Huguenot rebellion when the Saint Bartholomew massacre wiped out all of the family. So from 1572 down to the present day we have family history. Either in legend or in documents. The first document is from the city of Geneva, Switzerland. He's concerned with a man named Pierre Jain whom we have every reason to believe was the son of Claude, the first man who came, but who was not allowed inside as a citizen. He was outside the walls of Geneva which of course still stand. And the people who lived as he did were called inhabitants and they were not allowed the protection of the walls. But in due time his son was a citizen inside. And so this is family legend but the documents begin with Pierre. And this I think happens in 1695 is the first documented evidence. So there might have been two sons in between from Claude in 1572 to 1695. You see it leaves time for another generation in there. So as I recall the story and I have it in detail and could refer to it but we won't have time this morning. But anyway this William Benjamin who came to America with his family and here he had four more children. Grandmother giving birth to a child about two weeks after she arrived from that ship. And her mother-in-law, a very dear woman who lived in this beautiful manor, worried about them terribly. Because she said what will she ever do to keep those four lively boys busy on that tiny sailing vessel. She said I worry about what they'll become in that raw new country. They'll behave like nothing but savage Mohicans.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well did they ever tell you what she did do to keep those four boys?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: No there is no record of this. There is no record of this and no recollection that has ever been put down. But I can tell you that from another letter from this same worrying mother her concern was that William Benjamin changed his residences. Like some men changed their dirty shirts and he was a bottomless pit in the matter of having money sent to him.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I thought you said that he was somewhat ostracized for this marriage that he made.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: He wasn't ostracized he just didn't have any money from his wife.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: They just went dowry I see.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Yeah they just wanted any money and those men ordinarily had women who were so carefully picked you can't believe that I have copies of the marriage contract. They were saying exactly what these women would bring to the marriage in order to rape these gentlemen. And then the gentlemen in their turn their families said what they would do for this young couple. And it was one man Abraham sold some of his wife's dowry wedding presents which my cousin Paul Vous Bernier that put this family narrative together in March. And who translated his beautiful French into his faulty English which I have yet to get all straight into our kind of English American English. Said I think I like not this part of the family story because Abraham put into cash some of the lovely lemons and china and silver.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Everybody's got a little bit of black sheep.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: This man looks a little too greedy to Paul. Well anyway the only onus about the marriage to Rose Mennet was that by the way which was Menet. But we call it Rose Mennet was the fact that she didn't bring any money. But after 50 years of marriage her husband wrote to my grandpa saying and I have been privileged to live all these years to the finest woman in the whole wide world. So she merited a great deal. She lived through quite a lot of ups and downs. This is the fellow that came to America and this is the lovely Rose. She was sweet as she could be.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Those are really handsome pictures.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: I was looking this morning at this picture. This is the man who made money in the blockade of 1812. He was sent to England to make his fortune and his name is John Benjamin Jain Lemaire. His dates are 1774 to 1822. He made his money during that blockade in England and came back and bought this beautiful manor where Rose Jain was born. Which I guess is pertinent because it was his birthplace. It had beautiful grounds.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: He built Lemaire?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: No he bought it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: He bought it.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: He bought it at the edge of things where he'd have plenty of ground and where people wouldn't be dropping in all the time. According to his letters which we have.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: He made his money in the blockade of 1812 as an importer?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: He was an importer in England. His child was Jain Minnet. But John Benjamin Jain was born the 24th of April 1774 and baptized the 10th of May 1774 and died the 19th of July 1822.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Let me backtrack. Jain Mennet who?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Jain Minnet is this man who came to America. His grandson came to America. But it was in his home that the grandson was born. That's where he comes in. They all were somewhat interested in government. He was in the house of Burgess of Marge and Yens and Geneva. He took out English nationality in 1822 and became a Fremont of London. A member of the trade guild. Of the... it's a... Pavre... it's a salesman. And he married a very distinguished woman whose picture I have. And she goes back to the house of Blanchonnet which is the home or the location of the museum of Marge. And this is the picture of her ancestors who hang in the museum.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You showed me that one time.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Well, anyway. That's how that tangled... you're serving an awful lot here.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I'm trying at least. Yes. Okay.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Look at these generations of stuff. Who was the son of who and where and what.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Back to your grandfather, Lewis. He said he remembered the trip from Vernon to Boulder.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: That's my father.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Oh, your father remembered it, but your grandfather was the instigator of the trip.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: That's right.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Okay, well, what did your father ever tell you about the trip?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Nothing. We didn't talk about it. This is an interview with the paper which I'm so glad to have because Pete Peterson got busy and got him to talk. But, except for stories like I have interpolated there, I just don't have them. He's... you know, these things that you're always going to do and never get to. Well, we have Grandmother's diary where she talked about two instances that were of interest. Her diary turned on.

speaker: Speaker 2
words: It's on.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Oh, is it? Yes. Oh, Grandmother's diary would say, Lou shot a rabbit and we had it for supper. Or Lou was able to stop at such and such a store or post along the way and got us some crackers. Then for supper we had crackers and tea or crackers and milk. And they were never frightened by Indians and they came almost by themselves. The marks of the wheels of the wagons still are in the soil down at Mushad Flats. The other side... let's see, it would be east of the Massacre Rocks outside American Falls. They're 16 inches deep yet. And I think of Grandmother with four little kids and the dust that they must have lived in.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: They were by themselves.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Pretty much. I don't know whether there were three wagons or one wagon. I really think they were pretty independent going to the Willamette Valley over the Argon Trail. They didn't have... it certainly wasn't a train. And to think of the jolting of those hired old iron tires on the wheels for a woman who was not well and who had problems with digestion and hurt in her stomach and eventually wound up dead with cancer of the stomach. As long as I can remember when she would feel badly, she would send us children out to get clean snow from a snowbank. And she would munch the snow. It seemed to soothe her little hot tummy some way, you know. She was a delicate little thing.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you ever know from any source how she felt about coming west? It sounds like your grandfather really tended to move a lot.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Well, he had served in the Civil War and had gone...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Married maybe?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Yeah. Adelia Farwell and Lewis James were married, I think, February 14, 1866. In her father's home in Wisconsin. And I can't tell you exactly where that was. I know his name was Luke and her mother's name... Her stepmother's name was Mary Pope Farwell and her own mother was named, I think also Mary Beaulieu. B-E-A-U-L-I-E-U. And they have an incident that might be worth preserving. In the birth of Grace, their second... grandmother's second pregnancy, Grace was a twin. And grandmother was very ill and claimed the entire attention of the doctor and the neighbor woman who came to help. And grandfather saw the two little babies wrapped up on chairs and he took one little baby and put her in a pan of warm water and massaged her to get her to liven up a bit. And that was Grace and she lived. And little Gertrude, who was one more than anyone could give time to, died. And I thought that was a pathetic touch of pioneer living.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Where was that?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: That was in Wisconsin. That was grandmother's second pregnancy.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think your grandfather was a fairly restless man?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: No, he was not restless at all. He was driven by economic necessity, but he was a very solid, slow to decide, exceedingly strong character. He was so strong that it was welcome that he mellowed with age. His discipline was severe and instant and he brooked no interference. I think the necessities of his life dictated this kind of strength.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Why did he leave Wisconsin?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Oh, gee! As I think of it, they just had to have more land and more place to earn a living. He just couldn't support a family and they were getting children and he had to have larger opportunities. And some of the other brothers had moved. A man called Milas, who I suppose in English would be Miles, had gone to Colorado. And there were opportunities for mining there as well as for farming. And grandfather was just seeking to better their economic status. And then of course with the grasshoppers eating everything up, they were quote, starved out quote and had to leave.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: This was the big invasion of grasshoppers in Colorado.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Yes, there were two years hand running and they said they were so severe that they ate the handle of the hoe until it was snow white. In other words, every bit of the surface was chewed away. It was a terrible thing to endure. And two years hand running, all the crowds went. So of course they just couldn't support that. They had to go someplace where they could have a better chance. And of course the story of Oregon was fascinating to everybody. They wouldn't have gone if they hadn't had to.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well they never made it to Oregon though, did they?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Yes, they were in the Willamette and in the Indian Valley for one winter or two. And they took up some land there and I remember mother and father going on a trip back...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How they took up the land there?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: No, it just says they took up land. Now how it was I don't know. But that's from the right up here. The phrase was used, took up land. But they relinquished that apparently without any regrets and came on up to Genesee. It was very difficult for them at first. They nearly starved to death. But everyone was in the... To the first years in Genesee were very difficult. However, grandmother had a great spirit of fun in her heart. They said that there were so many single men, not very many white women and not very many families. And so they had a Christmas party in their tiny little dwellings of two rooms. And they invited every bachelor in the country. And grandmother, they couldn't provide presents for everybody and everybody just came in the clothes they stood in almost. So she had a present for every man there. She took a shoebox and made it into a railway coach. And out the window of each, on each side of the coach, there was a paper doll that was a bride for the bride. So each of them had a gift that came. The fun of it, you see. It didn't matter too much what they ate or too much what they did. It was the idea of the fun. She also participated in an incident which I think is charming and touching. Her neighbor, Mrs. Wall, had twins and was very ill. And grandmother was called as a neighbor women were to help. And she went over with her cherished sugar bowl full of Oregon grape jelly. It was a treasure she had brought with her. And people in great distress such as her neighbor Mrs. Wall were given a teaspoon of it, dissolved in water as a cordial. And that was really something.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Whenever I see the abundant Oregon grapes all over the country and think that we aren't too crazy about their flavor and put them with apple juice or grape juice or something to make them what we think is palatable, I look back at the time when those people thought they provided the greatest of treats. We have some interesting incidents in the family from the days when the whole family lived on the homestead which is at the top of the hill, eastern Genesee. And the trees can be seen from the whole wide countryside. Those trees, by the way, were planted in a timber culture for which grandfather got another 160 acres. And he planted hardwood trees thinking they were of greater worth than the quick growth of box elder or Lombardi chotler or whatever that were, cottonwoods, whatever other people planted for quick growth. He planted these hardwood trees which now are 100 years old and still are of quite small size because the soil is too rich for them. They're not suited to this black loam. Grandfather not only had the land that he bought in the beginning and preempted, or homestead or whatever he did, that I think accumulated to 206 and two-thirds acres. Then he got the timber culture and he also bought a school section. The story goes that he lost the school section after the Panic of 1893 because he owed his hired man a Mr. Houchier, and gave him the school section. This is the type of man that grandfather was and that his people were because he would pay even at great cost because the man who built the manor, John Benjamin James, had a partner in England who absconded with their money and he lost the manor and died of broken heart in 1822. His wife had her own money which fortunately could see the family through, but she had a large family of which my great grandfather was about the fourth child. Anyway, grandfather gave up the school section which presently is part of the Hampton Driscoll land.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well don't you think that this sort of trustworthiness and honesty was really one of the major factors in sort of pioneering life and time? Most people had that.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Oh yes, and I think this kind of integrity and this kind of sterling quality and adherence to the principles was something which they prized along with their independence. They loved to be generous, but when they couldn't be generous they could be very self-denying in achieving their goals. There's an amusing story. Grandfather planted fruit trees and had a beautiful orchard and I think there's one tree still stands. And one time a family whose fortune is very great these days, that had two boys and two girls, came by on the fourth of July morning early and bought the fruit on a pie cherry tree. There were several there, so grandfather just sold fruit on a whole tree to this neighbor whose name I will keep private. And they stripped the tree. They went down to a fourth of July celebration which was on the Clearwater River near Spalding. And the two half-grown teenage boys just kneeled down on their handsome knees and some boards were placed on their shoulders. And the cook stove which was on the back of the wagon was fired up and the neighbor's wife began rolling out pie dough and baking cherry pies. She didn't worry about pitting them, but she made those cherry pies and they smelled wonderful and they must have tasted pretty good because people came by and bought them at $8 a piece. And she did just fine. And the dollar, you see, was equal to a whole day's wages. So presently if we were to give $25 or $30 for a pie you would see something of the comparative cost of that pie. But of course it was a day of celebration. Now if somebody had his dollar I suppose he bought his pie. But for that family it wasn't a day of celebration, it was a day of profit. From that family we also have a story of their younger daughter herding the family pigs to pasture over on the Camas Prairie which is on the flats toward Grangeville. And then bringing them home and of course she walked and drove the pigs the whole way and she was just a slip of a girl, quite a nice looking girl. And she of course had to camp as she went and carry her supplies as she went. And she got over to a draw just east of our house with her pigs bringing them home in the fall and bedded down with them. And when the family came home from town here she was asleep alongside the road with her herd. That seemed rather pathetic because she married well and she was a dear girl and some of her relatives still are around. But that was how hard that family worked. The sacrifices they made to earn money.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well now this thing that your grandfather broke sod for two-fifteen acres, wouldn't that give him an opportunity to get ahead by doing that?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: They said it was quite a handsome return and they liked it very much but of course at two and a half an acre, even if he did a hundred acres, and I have no idea how much he did do, I suppose that wouldn't stretch over too long a time. But of course he was working the year round, he was doing everything men do to earn money on a farm in the 90s. This business of breaking sod, the punch grass was so abundant and so wonderful. But there was need of timber and lumber and my dad as he grew would freight lumber from the mill to people who needed it in Tennessee. And dad also had a meat business and he would take his fresh and nicely butchered meat under as ideal conditions as he could arrange and he would go to his customers far and right over the country to sell the meat. That lasted for some time and paid very well.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You were also talking about the prairie chickens. One thing I hadn't been able to determine was how much the early homesteaders and early settlers depended on the things that were already present in nature to make a living. Like prairie chickens or camas.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: They didn't ever use camas that I know of, but they certainly used the wild game. Prairie chicken, grouse, I don't know that there was any pheasant. There was quail and I think the pheasants were an import. But grouse, prairie chickens and quail doves all were eaten. And domestic pigeons were introduced and my mother could make a most delicious pigeon pot pie. And I don't know, a bear was eaten when it was shot up in the timber when people homesteaded. Grandfather Lanphear, while they lived at Hog Meadows, had a lot of pioneer lore with William Deary and the Bovills. And one touching story from the Bovill time was that the family came up from the farm and stayed all night at Hog Meadows. And there was a dance at the Bovills Hotel. And my sister was just a little girl, but the Bovill girls were also about her age. And they sat in the corner of the room at the dance. And there were those two families and the Warrens. And about seven men from a railroad construction, surveying rather than construction, surveying crew there for the party. And my sister said she sat on a trunk with the Bovill girls and they thought how beautiful it was when the lady's skirts whirled in the steps of the dance. The little girls came on horseback to see my sister out at Hog Meadows. And there's another cute little story about people would stop to eat with anyone who was along the way because there were no restaurants. And no one turned someone away from the door who was hungry because how awful it would be because he'd have to go for hours until he'd get to the next place where somebody might possibly have food for him. So Grandmother fed lots of people. And this one youngster was a growing boy and his mother was Irish. Well he ate and ate of Grandmother's food and thought it was so good that he just practically couldn't put down another bite. And then came the pie. And he looked at it and the tears came to his eyes because oh he just couldn't eat that pie. But his mother reached over and tapped his arm lovingly and said, stunned up you yeeganated patrick. They loved that little story. We have a picture of Grandmother at the pump at Hog Meadows and she was in working dress so I know that that was a busy time for her. They did very well there however. Grandfather located people on claims and I have his compass with the same rawhide thong on it from when he located people on claims. All through the Derry, Bovill area.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Maybe next time we'll go more into the Lanphears. Because there's a whole lot of questions I'd like to ask about that. Right now I think we get into that. I'm going to get really confused. How about the, you mentioned briefly the 1893 wet harvest. I wonder specifically what your Grandfather, Grandfather Jain, did. Grandfather Jain could weather the 1893 wet harvest as well as anyone did which wasn't very well.

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: But my father was, it was just very difficult because you can imagine no income for a year. What happened was they grew fine crops but before they could get them harvested the rains just kept coming and coming and coming until there was no harvesting. And the wheat sprouted in the shocks, sprouted in the stacks and simply was unmanageable and robbed and ruined. That's what happened in 93. That's when the man who became Governor McConnell or had been Governor McConnell and Senator McConnell already lost his holdings and went broke.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well what did your father do? Did they ever tell you the particulars of the adaptations they had to make to get through those?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: I don't know. He got married in 93 so apparently they made it alright. They probably had plenty to eat, you see. They would have, they probably weren't dependent on the wheat for food. But they and Dad would have meat and you know in those days there were pit vegetables just as there are now of cabbage and onions and rutabagas and that sort of vegetable. But they didn't worry too much about vegetables and fruit as I told you from that meal which my grandparents thought was perfectly good which was fat and well it was carbohydrates entirely. However I know a woman now who lives a very full and productive life and hasn't lived on anything but carbohydrates ever since I've known her which is going into 25 years. She regurgitates immediately anything that isn't a carbohydrate.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did your father ever talk to you or your grandfather or anything about what would be the determining factor between who made it and who didn't like somebody who came out at the same time possibly as your grandfather did?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: To have a little money with you and to have a little money for a backlog was just absolutely marvelous. If you didn't it came out of your endurance and the strength of your heart and some people fell to the wayside just as they always have in economic crises. I saw this myself personally in the depression. Some people went under from things that happened to them and then there were others who could endure. But in economic crises a little backlog is certainly helpful. So some people didn't have it and didn't seem to have to have it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How much do you think neighborliness played a factor?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Oh, neighborliness created a great factor in that people loved each other just as human beings always have and would help each other out particularly when there was sickness. Families helped each other, were very supportive through sickness periods. When my dad had the illness before Don was born in the Bundy House in Genesee between the 80 acres and the Catholic Gulch purchase of location, his parents and my mother's parents all came at various times to help care for him because he was exceedingly ill, critically ill for weeks, fun weeks.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Would the neighbors then, not just the family, would the neighbors come in and help out too?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Oh yes, we have a sweet story along this line if you're interested of a neighbor girl who was pregnant and was not married. Well, she got married I guess before the baby came and I won't say the name of these people because they're living in this area. And Mother felt very sorry for her. She was a sweet girl and this was only her own beloved to whom she gave her love. So Mother helped her make her leit and it was run up in a hurry as the expression goes but they made it in time to welcome the baby which was born right along that time. Great kindness, great generosity. They shared the materials and they shared effort and they shared their love. And as long as Mama lived she helped that woman there because you love as much the person you help as the person who is helped loves the supportive friend. And always there's been a special bond.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What other instances can you recall of community working together?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Oh, let me think. I'm sure there were a zillion but I just put in this context, I can't tell you just at the moment. Oh, when my mother had to go to Troy during my grandmother Lanphear's illness, I was sent to Charles and Jesse's home to be babysat so that Mother would be free to go and take care of her mother. And I remember the stay in the relatives' homes at home as a very precious incident. The snow was high and we made snow cream with... Oh, help me, I lost something. I just can't... Oh, that was great treatment. And the daughters were, it was so snowy and miserable that they didn't go to school and they stayed at home for some weeks. And Aunt Jesse taught them. This was Ansel and Olive, their daughters. There was a plant there that gave me my first lesson in botany because overnight we would put a pencil with a mark as to where the plant was. It was a fern. And the next morning we'd see how far it had grown during the night. And I actually witnessed, almost saw a plant grow. And so I knew it really did happen. There was the most delicious smell in Aunt Jesse's, for anyone's, root cellar. Because that's where the pickles were and the hams hung. And the root vegetables were stored. And oh my, that was a treasure house to go to to get the food for the next meal.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How about shared work? Did people work together quite a bit?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Yes, I'm sure they did. Particularly in families. And also even up into the Depression, people shared in the way of labor. I know in the small Depression that came after World War I, my sister was a bride. And they had a neighborhood threshing outfit which worked to great disadvantage for them. Because the people were, this was north of Tennessee, and the people were of Scandinavian extraction. They loved coffee. So that she made coffee in the wash boiler. And they grew this crop and the fellows came to harvest it and it started to rain. Well some of them didn't have very good provender at home, so they just stayed. And their team stayed. And it was the custom that you boarded the harvesters and they waited out the rain. Well these rains were excessive and by the time the season was over, there was no crops left.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Because they'd eaten the whole thing up?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: They'd eaten, they and their animals had eaten the whole thing up. Some men took pee and went home. But it was only the ones that were thoughtful enough to do so or kind enough to do so. So it worked in reverse as well as otherwise.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think people tended to get together more for enjoyment too?

speaker: Grace Jain Wicks
words: Yes, they had to make their own fun. I was too young to be in on country dances. I never went to a school program or anything like that because I was in town and not of the age. But the older children remember all of these things. Gladys McGee would be a real good one to interview. She lives at 1103 Prospect, Lewiston. She'd remember a lot of things I don't. And of course we'd remember things differently. And as to who is right, accurate, you can just take your guess.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: That's always very interesting to do.
